data.title: Celestica Still Has A Lot More To Offer
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Celestica Inc. (NYSE:CLS) has transformed into an under-the-radar enabler of the AI infrastructure buildout, hence the reason I am giving the company a strong buy rating.

I have previously covered other AI and semiconductor players such as Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd. (CRDO), Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL), Astera Labs, Inc. (ALAB), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) and Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI).

Celestica plays a key role in the AI supply chain by building and delivering complete hardware systems such as AI servers, network switches and cooling units. For context and to better understand where CLS fits in the AI ecosystem, let's consider the following: Companies like Credo and Astera Labs focus on tiny chips that help data move quickly between processors (of course, using retimers, CXL and SerDes chiplets). And then TSM and Micron make the actual chips and memory.

So where does CLS fit in all these?

Well, Celestica puts all these parts together into fully working machines. And if I were to compare with SMCI, which sells ready-made servers, CLS works more closely with big tech and hyperscalers to design custom hardware from the ground up, giving it a more strategic role.

As mentioned, as part of my larger AI infra research, I analyzed several companies that operate at different layers of the ecosystem. In my CRDO article titled "Credo is becoming a key standard in AI Interconnect," I discussed and provided details on how CRDO uses its Active Electrical Cables (AEC) and SerDes chiplets to help AI servers communicate faster at the required speeds. I also highlighted the importance of ultra-low latency interconnects that serve as the "brain" for AI workloads. And the important part is that these high-speed SerDes and AEC cables ultimately feed into the kind of networking systems that Celestica assembles.

Now when I come to Astera Labs, I focus on CXL and PCIe-based memory coherence, and this is a trend that will definitely shape next-gen AI server architecture. Again, the importance is that this is the kind that CLS is helping hyperscalers design and deploy.

Thirdly, my SMCI coverage emphasized their rapid server ramp and off-the-shelf AI hardware offerings, which contrast with CLS's customized offerings.

For Marvell Technology, I examined the switch ASIC and custom silicon programs that CLS integrates into its 800G and 1.6T (soon to come) platforms. In TSM, we covered how foundational semiconductor manufacturing capacity is enabling broader AI growth. And this capacity ultimately flows into systems built by integrators like CLS. My article on Micron Technology explored the rising demand for HBM and DRAM memory, which are key components for memory-intensive AI workloads deployed on the rack-scale systems CLS delivers.

And lastly, my research on POET Technologies Inc. (POET) explored how optical interposers can redefine data movement within AI servers by replacing copper with light. While POET remains in early commercialization, innovations like theirs could become part of future switch and server designs.

Now that I have mentioned most of the component innovators, you will notice that one thing stands out: AI is creating a huge demand for new hardware infrastructure. And this does not matter where the company fits. Whether it's making chips, improving data transfer, speed or building entire servers. They are literally helping in building the powerful systems and engines that power AI. And as I have demonstrated companies like Celestica that build the final systems used in AI data centers stand the chance to benefit more.

And that's why I am rating Celestica a Strong buy because I believe it is well-positioned and already working directly with the biggest tech companies to build and deliver custom AI hardware.

Growth Drivers

First and foremost, one of the key growth drivers is CLS hyperscaler partnerships. As of writing this, CLS has leveraged deep and meaningful relationships with some of the largest cloud customers. According to Robert Mionis, CEO, he notes that

our enterprise end market, we continue to expect our next generation AI/ML compute program with a large hyperscale customer to begin ramping mass production volumes in the third quarter, leading to our resumption of sequential growth in the second-half ...

... The market share gains we've achieved with hyperscale customers through awarded programs are expected to continue driven by our strong and expanding pipeline of opportunities.

This shows you that the company is gaining a significant market share with all its major hyperscalers especially when supplying the 400G, 800G and the 1.6T Ethernet switches. Again I note that the CCS segment revenue was reported at $1.84Bn which represents a 28% increase. And this was driven by strong growth for networking switches.

CCS segment revenue was $1.84 billion, up 28%, driven by very strong growth for networking switches in our communications and market. The CCS segment accounted for 70% of total revenue in the quarter.

Mandeep Chawla, CFO, 1Q FY2025.

Secondly, according to a report by McKinsey & Company, Generative AI could create additional value potential above what could be unlocked by other AI and analytics. Now, this means that the demand for infrastructure to support Gen AI will grow even faster. Obviously, companies will need powerful, customized data center infrastructure to support them.

When I look at CLS's growth grade, the company scores a B growth grade. From the figure below, I note that the company is not just growing but it is also outdoing its sector median. For instance revenue growth y/y grew at 21.05% while the sector median just did 6.24%, which represents 237% higher. And this is why I believe that Celestica is gaining market share in high-value segments like AI infrastructure.

In terms of the 3-5 yr CAGR GAAP EPS Growth, the company stands at 40.13% versus 11.06% sector median. Again, CLS is growing its earnings much faster than its peers. And this indicates margin expansion and strong cost control. I also believe this sets up a favorable PEG ratio as growth is high while valuation remains reasonable as I will demonstrate in the valuation section.

Valuation

In the last 2 years, the stock has increased by more than 1,000% as shown below. And this is due to the fact that the investor recognized its earnings inflection and AI exposure since 2023.

When I look at the FWD EV/Sales multiple, CLS is trading at 1.76x while the sector median stands at 3.09x which implies that the stock is 43% undervalued. On the other side, its FWD Price/Sales shows a 46% undervaluation. While Celestica trades at a multiple of 1.68x, the sector median stands at 3.13x. Lastly, its FWD PEG ratio is still below the sector median of 1.88x. At 1.3x, the stock still exhibits a 44% undervaluation.

In terms of peer comparison, as I had earlier mentioned, SMCI is the closest rival and that's why I decided to use it to compare with CLS. As I had mentioned, SMCI focuses on readymade AI servers while CLS focuses on custom-made servers and hardware. Nevertheless, a quick look at SMCI and I note that both companies are almost similar in terms of valuation numbers. Similar to CLS, SMCI is also undervalued in terms of the non-GAAP FWD PEG ratio by 41%. Yet, their growth grades are both high at B for CLS and B+ for SMCI.

Celestica Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for Celestica is customer concentration. There is a significant portion of CLS's revenues that is concentrated in a few large customers. For instance, in 1Q FY2025 the top 10 customers made over 78% of revenues and also implied that the top 2 [which are likely Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META)] each account for over 10%.

"...During the quarter, we had three customers that each accounted for at least 10% of total revenue, representing 28%, 13%, and 10% of revenue respectively..."

Delays or reduction in Capex expenditure could reduce its orders which could in turn reduce revenue projections and future growth projections.

Final Thought

Despite the stock returning more than 1000% in the last 2 years, Celestica still has a lot to offer. The company has great growth prospects. It is increasing its partnerships with major hyperscalers and is operating in an explosive market that is dominating the current AI ecosystem. Its FWD Price/sales and non-GAAP FWD PEG ratio indicate that the company's stock could be significantly undervalued. And for these reasons, I rate Celestica a strong buy.

data.title: Bank OZK: Robust Loan Growth Offsets Net Interest Margin Weakness
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Bank OZK (OZK) has outperformed the iShares U.S. Financials ETF (IYF), delivering a gain of 20%:

I previously covered Bank OZK with a Buy rating in April 2025, highlighting the bank's attractive valuation relative to regional bank peers. The bank's Q2 2025 financials confirm my bullish thesis, with EPS increasing Y/Y despite a decline in the net interest margin.

With 1% in Fed rate cuts priced over the next year, EPS growth at Bank OZK will likely remain muted. Even so, I confirm my Buy rating for the common shares as they trade at a discount to both regional banks as well as broader U.S. financials held in the IYF ETF.

I also reaffirm my Buy rating on the bank's 4.625% fixed-rate preferred shares (OZKAP) which offer robust dividend coverage and a total return potential of about 10% over the next 12 months.

Company Overview

You can access all Bank OZK results on its website here. Bank OZK is a U.S. regional bank focused on real estate financing - its Real Estate Specialties Group /RESG/ accounts for 60% of all loans, followed by Community Banking at 16% of loans, with Indirect Recreational Vehicles /RV/ and Corporate & Institutional Banking /CIB/ each accounting for 12% of loans:

Total assets are $41.4 billion, of which 13% are funded with common equity (at book value), 1% with preferred equity (at par), with the remainder coming from customer deposits.

93% of the bank's Q2 2025 revenue came from net interest income - a key concern in light of anticipated Fed rate cuts.

Q2 2025 Results Overview

Bank OZK reported EPS of $1.58/share, up 3.9% Y/Y. The key drivers behind the profit increase were lower provisions, marginally stronger net interest income (driven by loan growth), an 8.7% Y/Y increase in non-interest income, as well as a 0.7% decrease in shares outstanding:

Given the uncertain U.S. macroeconomic backdrop, it is quite encouraging to see Bank OZK reporting lower provisions both Q/Q and Y/Y. I was also positively surprised by the uptick in the net interest margin to 4.36% (+0.05% Q/Q), although it remains 0.32% lower Y/Y, reflecting the effect of 1% of Fed rate cuts late in 2024, partially offset by lower deposit funding costs:

Outlook for Fed policy

Looking at Fed futures prices, we observe that the market currently prices in another 1% in rate cuts to 3.25-3.50% in July 2026, potentially indicating Bank OZK's net interest margin will be only marginally above 4% in July 2026:

As we have seen this year, retained earnings and robust loan growth (loans were up 15% Y/Y in Q2 2025) should allow Bank OZK to absorb the negative impact of lower interest rates, with EPS growing at a low single-digit rate. What is more, Fed rate cuts will make servicing/refinancing real estate debt easier, potentially indicating recent declines in credit loss provisioning should continue over the next few years.

Valuation

Bank OZK trades at just 7.8x its analyst consensus EPS of $6.54/share for 2026:

However, as highlighted above, I only expect Bank OZK to achieve a low single-digit EPS increase in 2026. This puts the analyst consensus earnings growth of 7% a bit high in my opinion. The silver lining is that even if we factor in lower EPS growth in 2026 to about $6.20/share, we still see that the bank is trading at 8.4x its 2026 earnings.

For comparison, U.S. financials held in the IYF ETF trade at 16.8x their trailing earnings, while regional banks held in the SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) trade at 12.1x their forward earnings. As such, despite Bank OZK's strong year-to-date returns, I think a Buy rating is still warranted for the common shares.

The Preferred Shares

Bank OZK's 4.625% fixed-rate preferred shares (NASDAQ:OZKAP) currently offer a dividend yield of ~6.77% and significant capital appreciation upside as they trade at a circa 31% discount to their par value of $25/share.

Bank OZK paid $4 million in preferred stock dividends in Q2 2025, just 2% of the lender's net income before preferred distributions. What is more, preferred equity of $339 million is covered by common equity market capitalization 17 times. Both metrics indicate a high safety profile for OZKAP's distributions, with ongoing EPS growth likely to increase preferred share dividend coverage in the years ahead.

I think that with 1% of Fed rate cuts potentially on the horizon over the next year, it is not unreasonable to assume that some of the capital appreciation upside discussed in my previous article will be realized over the next year. Back in April 2025, I estimated a fair value of OZKAP around $17.80/share, which I think is somewhat conservative in light of ongoing robust EPS growth.

Then again, we need to mention the bank's high cost of common equity, which arguably keeps a lid on preferred share valuations. All in all, I still believe a Buy rating is warranted for OZKAP, with investors potentially achieving a total return of around 10% over the next 12 months, driven by a current yield of 6.77% and a capital gain of about 4% to my fair value target.

Risks

The principal risk facing investors is that the U.S. economy is going through a below-trend period of growth, with the Fed expecting 1.4% GDP growth in 2025 and 1.6% in 2026, below the 1.8% growth rate expected in the long term:

As such, while credit loss provisions have been kept in check in H1 2025, there is no certainty that they will not tick up later this year and into 2026. That said, I do expect Bank OZK's focus on real estate financing to be a net benefit relative to other regional banks, at least as long as the Fed cuts interest rates as priced in by the markets.

Buyers of OZKAP preferred shares should note that the capital gains are hard to predict with certainty, with dividend yields relative to risk-free interest rates (the yield spread) affected by a number of factors such as financial market stability, trends in regional bank performance, etc. As such, reaching my $17.80/share price target is not guaranteed even if the Fed largely follows the path priced in by futures markets.

Conclusion

Bank OZK delivered a 3.9% Y/Y increase in Q2 2025 quarterly EPS, benefitting from robust loan growth, higher net interest and non-interest income, lower provisions, and a smaller share count.

Looking ahead, the bank will likely have to absorb another 1% in Fed rate cuts by July 2026, indicating EPS growth will remain muted as the central bank ploughs ahead with its monetary policy normalization.

Even so, I believe Bank OZK common shares remain a buy as they trade at a steep discount relative to both regional banks as well as the broader U.S. financials sector. More conservative investors may want to consider the fixed-rate preferred shares, which offer a total return of about 10% over the next 12 months. This is quite attractive given the robust preferred dividend coverage, earning OZKAP a buy rating on my part.

Key risks include U.S. GDP growth running below trend in 2025-2026, potentially signaling elevated credit losses, as well as the uncertain dynamic between Fed rate cuts and required rates of return on Bank OZK preferred shares.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: QuantumScape: Pump The Brakes
data.description:
QuantumScape Corporation (NYSE:QS) has gone parabolic after announcing the anticipated release of new technology for solid-state batteries. The stock has more tripled now on the news in weeks, though the initial production testing won't take place until 2026. My investment thesis is still Neutral on the stock, with the opportunity to own already quickly passing.

Commercial Push

About 3 weeks ago, QuantumScape announced the successful integration of the advanced Cobra separator process into baseline battery cell production. In essence, the SSB technology made another major step towards commercial launch of their solid-state lithium-metal battery technology.

The Cobra separator offers an ~25x improvement to the prior Raptor process in heat treatment speed and occupies a fraction of the physical space per film start. These key advantages are expected to lead to a scalable gigafactory production line.

Back on the Q1'25 earnings call, CEO Siva Sivaram highlighted the expected introduction of the Cobra separator during the Q2 timeframe as follows:

Our first key goal for the year is to bring the Cobra separator process into baseline production. This project is ahead of schedule. All the required separator processing equipment has already been installed and qualification is progressing well. We expect to bring Cobra into the baseline in Q2.

While Cobra offers a huge improvement in heat treatment, QuantumScape still only plans to release high-volume QSE-5 B1 samples later this year. The 2026 plan involved the intent to implement a low-volume but high-visibility commercial test with the launch customer over multiple phases to verify the ability to scale production of the SSB technology from QuantumScape.

Anyone following the SSB segment knows the benefits of these batteries are as follows:

  • Energy Significantly increases volumetric and gravimetric energy densities by eliminating graphite/silicon anode host material

  • Fast Charge Enables <15-minute fast charge (10-80%) by eliminating lithium diffusion bottleneck in anode host material
  • Life Extends useful lifetime by eliminating capacity loss at anode interface
  • Safety Eliminates organic separator and replaces with a solid-state separator that is nonflammable and noncombustible
  • Cost Lowers cost by eliminating anode host material and manufacturing costs

The big issue sort of blown off by management in the earnings call is that BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) and CATL in China have both announced more traditional EV batteries with quick charging times. As an example, BYD's Super e-Platform can add 249 miles (400 km) of range in just 5 minutes of charging time using 1,000 kW charging speeds. In addition, the Blade Battery has up to 5,000 charge and discharge cycles.

On the Q1'25 earnings call, the company was dismissive of the battery technology advancements. If BYD and CATL continue making battery advancements, SSBs won't have the huge upside promised a few years ago, with standard lithium-ion batteries used in EVs having extended charging times and questionable lives.

Unsustainable Rally

QuantumScape has soared to $13.60 pushing the market cap to $7.6 billion. While the Cobra launch increases the odds of commercialization, the VW deal does limit the upside due to the reduced capex and focus on royalty revenues.

The consensus analyst estimates are very minimal until 2028 and beyond. The forecasts only have a combined revenue of $65 million through 2027 and these estimates were based on forecasts for the Q2 Cobra announcement.

At this point, QuantumScape has 3+ years of work moving from test samples to production with partners in gigafactory battery production lines in order to start generating the revenue worthy of the current stock price.

The company has a large cash balance of $860 million, but QuantumScape forecasts producing a $250 to $280 million adjusted EBITDA loss for the year. The cash balance will quickly disappear with any hiccup in the business plan of commercializing the service next year.

The upcoming Q2'25 earnings report on July 23 has the potential to derail the current rally. The market has already priced in massive commercial success and management is likely to reinforce the slow test schedule over the next year after all the Cobra separator introduction was late coming at the very end of the quarter.

The company won't boost any revenue targets from the latest developments. The major hope is another automotive OEM customer.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that QuantumScape still has a long path to commercial success. After the big rally, the stock no longer offers a major value and could quickly reverse the recent gains. Investors don't necessarily need to sell the stock as the recent momentum appears relentless and likely drives the stock higher in the short term, but the commercial risks won't support a higher price.

data.title: Making Sense Of Palantir's Astronomical Valuation
data.description:
The data analytics powerhouse Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) is offering solutions to governments and the commercial sector. By using their powerful software, they turn individual non-contiguous data sets into actionable insights for their customers

The company's stock price has performed phenomenally in the last two years, capturing the attention of private and institutional investors. This raises the question: Have Palantir's fundamentals also improved phenomenally, or has the stock run too hot?

My Coverage

From my last article in October 2024, Palantir is up by more than 250%.

While I was bullish on the company and its prospects, I was skeptical if they could deliver the growth and profitability metrics needed to justify the enormous valuation. At the time, the company was trading at a PE ratio of 240 paired with a PS of 40. This led to required top-line growth rates of 35.5%as well as the need for constant profitability improvements in the future.

At that point Palantir was coming from decelerating growth rates and was just slowly shifting to positive EBIT margins, justifying my cautious rating.

Financial year 2021 2022 2023
Revenue 1,542 1,905 2,225
Revenue CAGR 41% 24% 17%
EBIT -411 -161 120
Operating Margin -27% -8% 5%

In this article, we are going to revamp the valuation to see if the company offers more upside right now or if the valuation might even worsened despite improving fundamentals.

Valuation

On a forward-looking basis, we currently have similar levels in the PE ratio compared to October 2024 levels.

I don't need to tell you that these PE and PS levels are very elevated and hard to justify. Other metrics show similar levels leading to a valuation grade of F.

I however believe that we get a more fitting impression by analyzing the company through a Discounted Cash Flow Analysis. For this we need some initial assumptions:

Profitability: Like mentioned before, the company is constantly improving their EBIT margin. To adjust for this, I predicted the following margins for our timeframe. Their current adjusted EBIT margin (including stock-based compensation) is at 44%, which is why I assumed 45% as the max. GAAP EBIT margin.

Cash Conversion: For Q1/2025 Palantir achieved a Cash Conversion rate of 173% (of the EBIT). Taking this rate and the ones from 2021 to 2024 into account, an average rate of 150% seems fitting.

Discount Rate: Here I assumed a conservative rate of 10%, compared to their current WACC of ~8.5%.

Perpetuity Growth: 3.5%

With these metrics we can calculate the required growth rate needed to justify the current valuation of $351 billion.

Required Growth

Within our DCF, Palantir needs to achieve growth rates of >44% per year for eight years straight to justify their current market cap. This sound very high, but let's look at the possible growth rates of the underlying market.

Market Growth

Experts are currently predicting the following growth rates for the AI market in the following years:

30.6% Markets and Markets
26.6% Statista
35.9% Grand View Research
29.2% Fortune Business Insights
37.3% Forbes

This gives us an average expected market growth rate of 32% per year. By just plugging this into our DCF, we get a fair value market cap of $175 billion, which indicates a potential overvaluation of up to 48%.

However predicting suitable growth rates for Palantir is very challenging. Their unique approach to AI consulting could offer higher or lower growth rates, mostly being reliant on competition and PLTR's execution.

Reasons to invest

Despite their stretched valuation, there are plenty of compelling reasons to still consider an investment in Palantir. First up, it is deeply integrated into the intersection of AI, data analysis as well as mission-critical decision making. Here the company is - as of now - one of the few that is successfully monetizing AI across key sectors like defense, healthcare, and energy, all while increasing profitability and accelerating their commercial revenue growth.

Additionally the company landed deeply entrenched government contracts across several countries from the west that provide a stable and especially recession proof cash flow base line.

Therefore Palantir could very well grow into their valuation or even exceed the needed growth rates. Funnily enough, as of Q1/2025 Palantir's YoY revenue growth rate was spot on, as they exactly achieved the required growth rate of 44%.

Reasons to be cautious

The current valuation, required growth, and needed profitability improvement leave little to no room for error. The company needs to execute flawlessly, scale their revenue and customer base significantly while also constantly improve their profitability. Any slow-down in growth or political headwinds could send the stock on a downward spiral.

Much of the recent price run-up - or to be honest their valuation in general - is as of now driven by AI enthusiasm and the potential growth prospects rather than tangible improvements in fundamentals.

While their commercial growth is improving, more than 50% of the current revenue is still generated via government contracts. Like mentioned these are stable, but also offer limited margin expansion and scalability.

Additionally, the company's aggressive stock-based compensation plans have been consistently diluting shareholders over the last years.

stock based comp. 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
[billion $] 1.27 0.78 0.56 0.48 0.69

On top of that, the AI space as a whole is becoming increasingly competitive, especially through massive players like Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN) that could pour billions into stealing market share from Palantir.

Conclusion

Palantir needs to achieve revenue growth rates of at least 44% per year to justify their current valuation. They additionally need to constantly improve their EBIT margin.

By anticipating the projected market growth rate of the underlying AI market (32% p.a.), the company could be overvalued by up to 48%. This leaves little to no room for any errors in execution.

One thing, however, is certain: Should Palantir continue to grow at this rate and therefore execute their growth flawlessly, they will become one of the largest - if not the largest - company in the world.

I, personally, have already trimmed my initial position - bought at ~$11 - three times and realized several 100% gains by doing so. Considering this and Palantir's unique position in a promising market as well as recent growth and profitability improvements, I plan on keeping every single share. I will only sell if growth decelerates significantly or the general story changes.

With all this in mind I currently rate Palantir a Hold. Investors who haven't realized any gains might want to use these prices to trim their position.

What's Your Perspective? Let's Discuss!

Thank you for reading my analysis! I'd love to hear your ideas and points of view—whether you agree, disagree, or have a unique take on the stock. Let's start a discussion in the comments!

data.title: Joby's Takeoff Has Just Started
data.description:
Investment Thesis

On its face, Joby Aviation (NYSE:JOBY) could appear to some as another SPAC-era moonshot eVTol name struggling through a high-burn, low-revenue existence. But that storyline ignores the greater structural play underway.

In an era of escalated geopolitics involving reshoring, decarbonization, and reindustrialization, Joby is not simply designing electric aircraft; it is reverse-engineering the very DNA of American aerospace for the coming century. Its true asymmetry is not premised on urban mobility buzz, but instead its diverging business roles as an integrated aerospace factory, first-of-a-kind FAA certification trailblazer, and as an integrated quad-play, dual-use multinational platform business enjoying secular tailwinds underpriced by most on the Street.

What makes that especially contrarian is that as peers compete for headlines with spec pre-orders, Joby has assembled five full-scale aircraft, flown repeated piloted transitions, achieved fault-tolerant vertical landings with disabled rotors, and has advanced FAA conforming aircraft in pipe.

Behind the headlining Dubai flight tests is an even bigger wager: Joby is operationalizing a Toyota-like manufacturing system at the confluence of aerospace and autonomy, with direct access to commercial, defense, and overseas markets. It's not just an urban taxi prototype; they're building a new layer of aviation, fashioned out of vertical integration and capital discipline, that could redefine the CapEx economics of flight itself.

From Hype to Hardware: Why Joby's July Rally Signals a New Phase of Execution

This week's bump up in Joby Aviation's stock by nearly 50% was fueled by a string of concrete operational developments that constituted an inflection point shift from prototype development to production readiness. Centerpiece of these developments is its expansion of its Marina, California factory, doubling its capacity to 24 aircraft annually.

Meanwhile, Joby also confirmed that its Dayton, Ohio factory is gearing up to accommodate a scalable infrastructure that can produce as many as 500 aircraft annually. This transition reconfirmed investor optimism that Joby is no longer merely iterating back in the lab, the company is building the industrial infrastructure to scale.

Moreover, Joby reiterated that its commercial operation would start in Dubai by early 2026, citing advances in pilot-on-board transition flights and the continued development of a vertiport in Dubai International Airport. This announcement did as much to cement the credibility of the company in completing overseas deployment timelines as did proof that its Middle Eastern approach is anything but aspirational.

Having also gained further impetus, Joby agreed to a memorandum of understanding with the Saudi business group of Abdul Latif Jameel for an envisaged $1 billion investment across aircraft purchase, operational service, and implementation of infrastructure. That gave institutional investors confidence in the durability and scale of Joby's global demand pipeline.

Another significant event to watch is the FAA certification movement, namely, entry from Stage 4 (Testing) into Stage 5 (Show & Verify), and first FAA-conforming aircraft test flight. Doing so in late 2025 or early 2026 might mark that inflection point that re-rates Joby from speculative tech to regulated commercial aviation platform.

Joby's Moment: The eVTOL Gold Rush is Real, and It's Already Underway

The eVTOL market is not some far-off scenario of science fiction, but an actual existing $1.35 billion market destined for $28.6 billion by 2030 growing 54.9% compound annual growth rate. This is not a buzz. It's the result of very real developments: battery density advances, regulatory tailwinds, urban density, and geopolitical necessity to decarbonize that for the first time since commercial jet travel has come together. The largest aerospace companies such as Airbus, EHang, Elbit, are investing in the space. But there is only one defensibly unique first-mover advantage: Joby Aviation.

While most companies are still prototyping, Joby is flying full-scale aircraft, completing pilot-on-board transitions, and logging FAA progress that dwarfs its peers. No other eVTOL firm has Joby's combination of:

  1. five built aircraft with military delivery,
  2. ongoing FAA-conforming production,
  3. $813 million in cash with Toyota as a strategic anchor, and
  4. a vertically integrated U.S. factory base poised to scale to 500 aircraft/year.

Inflection Point: Why the Rally May Just Be Getting Started

For investors thinking of holding, adding, or taking profits after the July explosive spike of Joby Aviation, the answer is not based on price movement but operating speed. Joby's Q1 2025 is the inflection point. It is a transition-phase business converting the roadmap into physical scale and regulatory delivery.

In Q1, Joby achieved its first-ever aircraft shipments to the U.S. DoD, began production of its first conforming aircraft, and accomplished several piloted flights out of its Marina facility. This wasn't symbolic development. It was structural. Beginning conforming aircraft production is an indicator of preparedness for FAA certification stage 4-5 transition. Alongside the switching on of Joby's vertically integrated factory base, the Company's path now resembles an aerospace original equipment manufacturer, rather than that of a startup.

Q2 is the quarter of judgment for investor confirmation for Joby. This latest rally in the stock has priced optimism, but Q2 is the first chance to confirm that the prototype-to-production transition of Joby is on believable lines. Earnings on 6th August, regulatory push, scale of production, and commercial preparedness are what everyone is watching, and not revenue or EPS surprises, which are largely symbolic going into this stage.

Then the market would look for movement toward FAA certification, i.e., into Stage 4 (Testing & Analysis) from Stage 3 (Cert Plans) by Joby and intimations of near-term movement into Stage 5 (Show & Verify). Any reference to component or system-level tests succeeding, or indeed tests of the existing FAA-conforming aircraft under assembly, would represent a significant de-risking event. No longer is the investor worried about whether or not Joby is going to fly, but by how soon and economically can it certify under FAA Part 23 and 135 rules.

Operationally, Q2 can also provide insight into the cadence of manufacture. As Marina has grown to 435,000+ sq. ft. and Ohio is outfitting its supply chain center, commentary around build rates and inventory-readiness will more effectively allow for an evaluation of when an acceleration from FAA certification to commercial scale is possible for Joby.

Valuation Reframed: A Milestone-Driven, Option-Like Framework for Joby

Classic valuation metrics such as forward P/S, P/E, or even DCF inherently do not reflect what JOBY is today. Joby is being valued as a milestone-clearing call option on the commercialization of an entirely new mode of transport.

Rather than basing multiples from revenue or earnings, the appropriate perspective is that of a staged probabilistic model, similar to valuing biotechs or defense contractors that are in pre-approval phases. Joby's value today is the aggregate of navigating critical operational, regulatory, and commercialization gates, each of which frees up a new tranche of potential upside. These are:

  1. FAA Certification (Part 23 + Part 135 integration)

  2. First commercial launch (Dubai 2026 or DoD contract ramp)

  3. scaled manufacturing execution (Marina + Dayton footprint)

  4. Vertiport and ecosystem buildout

  5. Recurring revenue from operations (not just aircraft sales)

Joby trades on milestone velocity, how quickly and credibly it advances along this de-risking path. Each step narrows uncertainty and raises the probability-weighted future cash flows, which is why news like aircraft conformity, DoD deliveries, and vertiport progress cause disproportionate stock reactions.

In that framework, Joby's current valuation is not revenue, at least, not yet. It's narrative credibility and execution visibility. The more on-schedule checkpoints (FAA flight tests, opening of Dubai, closing of Saudi contract), the more the equity re-grades from spec to strategic. Risk is not over-valuation. It's delayed or derailed. But if Joby is on schedule, its true upside is not multiple expansion, but opening up its category that does not exist on the traditional model.

Risks: Execution is Binary, Capital Intensity is High, and Narrative Can Outrun Readiness

Even though it has operational merits, there are also several threats for Joby. Certification slip is most noticeable. Pilot-on-board transfers as well as fault-tolerant tests are substantial, but Stage 4 of FAA Stage 4 is complete for only 43%, and Stage 5 only just started. Any slip-up in forcing FAA-compliant aircraft through test and validation would activate TIA flights after 2026, tightening investor tolerance and burn without offset revenues.

Moreover, vertical integration and long-run defensibility that are made possible by the CapEx of Joby generate short-run inflexibility. Pre-peak, the firm commits to spending on aircraft, simulators, and vertiports. It would struggle, for instance, if market prospects deteriorate, interest rates go up, or political support diminishes (especially in Middle Eastern countries), since the business model of Joby would end up being front-loaded and inflexible, even more if Toyota's second tranche of $250 million is slowed down or put on hold.

Despite being pre-revenue still, and posting an operating loss of Q1 2025 of $163 million, Joby has a comfortable cash balance of $813 million, providing enough runway to certification and first commercialization. Manufacturing and vertiport buildout capital intensity remains high, but liquidity is not short term. Risk is ultimately not investment, but execution slip or regulatory delay that would increase duration of burn. Volatility is story-driven, but as long as Joby does hit key development milestones, the capital model is bulletproof.

And, finally, there is story risk. There has been tremendous buzz among media and analysts about being a first-mover for Joby, but sentiment can turn on a dime very quickly if one of the certification obstacles is breached or there is an accident involving tests. Investors need to steel themselves for volatility, as sentiment has such a bearing on capital flows for an industry.

Takeaway

Joby isn't a speculative bet on urban air taxis; it's a precision-timed platform play at the convergence of autonomy, aerospace, and infrastructure. With FAA-conforming aircraft in build, global commercial launches underway, and Toyota-backed production scaling, Joby's valuation reflects not hype, but the monetization of certification milestones few others will reach.

data.title: Waller May Be Alone In Advocating July Rate Cut, But It Weighs On The Greenback
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar is trading softer against most G10 and emerging market currencies today. The dollar seemed to lose its bid late yesterday after Federal Reserve Governor Waller argued in favor of a rate cut at this month's meeting, despite the TIC data that showed foreign investors bought more US securities in May than they did in the first five months of 2024 ($311 bln vs $95 bln), driving home the message again that the talk of a capital strike against the US over its large deficit/debt or loss of "American exceptionalism" has been grossly exaggerated. The US announced a 93.5% tariff on graphite from China (given the other tariffs the effective rates is near 160%), which may have the effect of making more it more expensive to develop an EV battery industry in the US.

Equities are mostly firmer today after the S&P 500 (SPX, SP500) and Nasdaq (NDX) reached record highs yesterday. In the Asia Pacific region, among the large bourses, Japan, South Korea, and India failed to join the advance. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is extending yesterday's gain, which snapped a three-day slide. It is practically flat on the week. US index futures are steady to firmer. Japanese long bond yields softened slightly despite the measure of CPI that excludes fresh food and energy unexpectedly rose. There may have been some light position squaring ahead of Sunday's upper house election. European benchmark 10-year yields are up 1-3 bp to pare this week's declines. A notable exception is the 10-year Gilt, which has seen a nearly eight basis point increase this week. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is slightly softer, near 4.44%, which is up about three basis points on the week. Gold is firmer near $3353 but still within its recently well-worn range. August WTI is trading at its best level since Monday, near $68.50 following a new batch of EU sanctions on Russia and its oil.

USD: A favorable combination of US data yesterday of stronger than expected retail sales, softer import prices, and the fifth consecutive decline in initial weekly jobless claims extended the Dollar Index's recovery. It reached 98.95, the highest level since June 23, and rose through the upper Bollinger Band (~98.85 today) for the first time in two months. There has been no follow-through buying, and DXY is trading quietly lower. It has held below 98.60 and held so far above 98.30. We note that the North American session has seemed to be better for dollar buyers this week. The week winds down with June housing starts, and after a dramatic 9.8% drop in May, a modest rebound is expected. Economists will adjust residential investment projections that feed into Q2 GDP, which will be released on July 30. No fewer than a dozen Fed officials spoke this week and the Beige Book was published. There seems to be a broad agreement that the Fed's stance is appropriate, despite the pressure from the White House. Still, given Governor Waller's comments yesterday, it is possible he dissents from the most likely decision at the upcoming FOMC meeting to stand pat. The University of Michigan consumer confidence survey is due today. The assessment of current conditions and expectations may have softened. Economists polled by Bloomberg expect the one-year inflation outlook to remain elevated at 5%, which is not confirmed by other surveys or market measures. The 5–10-year inflation outlook may tick down to 3.9% from 4.0%. Recall at the end of last year, around the time of the Fed's last cut, the one-year inflation outlook was 2.8% and the longer projection was 3.0%.

Euro: After ending a six-day slide on Wednesday, the euro was sold again yesterday. It made a marginal new low for the month, slightly above $1.1555. Still, it has come back better bid today and is knocking on the $1.1645 area late in the European morning. Barring a recovery above $1.1690 today, it will be the first back-to-back weekly loss for the euro since mid-May. The Eurozone reported May's current account surplus was 32.3 bln euros. That puts this year's average monthly surplus near 25.1 bln euros. The average in the first five months of last year was about 36.3 bln euros. So, while the current account surplus has narrowed, the trade surplus has widened despite the increased penetration by China-based producers. The average monthly trade surplus this year has been about 18.55 bln euros, up from 17.33 bln euros in the Jan-May 2024 period. Separately, construction in the eurozone fell by 1.7% after rising a revised 4.3% in April (initially 1.7%), which was the strongest since May 2020. It is likely being flattered by government efforts and EU encouragement to modernize infrastructure.

CNY: The dollar recorded the low for the year on July 1 near CNH7.15. It has probed the CNH7.19 area on Wednesday and consolidated slightly below there yesterday. The high from June 23 is around CNH7.1925 and the greenback has not traded above CNH7.20 since June 11. A narrow range prevails today (~CNH7.1800-CNH7.1860). The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1498 (CNY7.1461 yesterday and CNY7.1475 a week ago). The dollar's fix yesterday was the lowest in nearly eight months (CNY7.1461) and the change, 0.09%, was the most in almost two months. Chinese officials continue to moderate the pressures emanating from the foreign exchange market. The yuan's rise against the dollar this year is modest (~1.6%). Beijing seeks not a strong or weak yuan but a stable one against the US dollar. It is not pegged to the US currency, as is the Hong Kong Dollar and the HKMA intervened earlier this week to defend the peg. Beijing does allow some movement in the yuan, and its chief tool remains setting the daily reference rate, around which the greenback is allowed to move 2%, but rarely does. Still, as we have noted, the daily fix was adjusted at the start of the year by about 0.01%. It has widened on average over the last several months, and while it is still quite small, it shows a little flexibility.

JPY: The dollar is firm against the Japanese yen. It has met sellers a little above JPY149 for the past three sessions. It has held below there today, but the consolidation seems constructive. Nearby resistance may be seen around JPY149.40, the halfway point of this year's range. Above there is the 200-day moving average (~JPY149.70), which the greenback has not traded above since mid-February. It is not a popular view, but we note that the rolling 30-day correlation of changes in the dollar-yen and the US 10-year yield is slightly above 0.80, the highest it has been since the end of 2021. Japan's June CPI was largely in line with the signal generated from the Tokyo CPI that was released a few weeks ago. The headline pace slowed to 3.3% from 3.5% and the core measure, which excludes fresh food, eased to 3.3% from 3.7%. The surprise was the measure that excludes fresh food and energy, which edged up to 3.4% from 3.3%, despite the slippage in the Tokyo reading (3.1% vs. 3.3%). Note that the data highlight next week is Tokyo's July CPI (July 25) and headline and core rates are expected to have ticked down. Still, the elevated price pressures and the relatively weak yen underpin the dissatisfaction with the government. The LDP-Komeito coalition lost their majority in the lower house last year and looks likely to lose the majority in the upper house in Sunday's election. A key programmatic difference between the coalition and leading opposition parties is over how to support households that are being squeezed by inflation. The coalition supports a cash handout, while the opposition advocates a cut in the sales tax.

GBP: While it is difficult to imagine a better string of economic data than the US reported yesterday, the opposite is true for the UK. Following last week's news of an unexpected contraction in May, the second consecutive month that the US economy shrank, it reported higher than expected CPI and weaker employment data in recent days. Sterling's eight-day slide was interrupted on Wednesday, but it resumed yesterday. Yet it held above Wednesday's low, near $1.3365 yesterday. Sterling's loss of about 0.10% yesterday put it atop of the G10 performances against the dollar. It appears to have been supported by the rise in the year-end expected rate to about 3.75% yesterday, the fourth gain in five sessions and the highest level since June 9. Yesterday's 5.5 bp increase was the most in almost two months. Trading is subdued, and sterling is in a narrow $1.3410-$1.3445 range so far today. A move above $1.3465 may help stabilize the technical tone.

CAD: The greenback forged a base this week near CAD1.3670 and reached a new high for the month yesterday around CAD1.3775 yesterday. It is trading with a softer bias today in a tight range between about CAD1.3725 and CAD1.3755. We have often found that when the US dollar is bid, the Canadian dollar tends to perform well on the crosses. This has indeed been the case this month. The Dollar Index bottomed on July 1, and the Canadian dollar is the best performing G10 currency against the US dollar so far this month, losing only a little more than 1%. At the same time, the market has pulled away from another rate cut by the Bank of Canada this year. The swaps market implies a year-end rate of 2.55% (2.75% currently), which is up almost 15 bp this month.

AUD: The disappointing Australian employment data helped send the Aussie to a new low for the month near $0.6455. This met the (61.8%) retracement of the rally from the June 23 low. It took out the potential neckline (~$0.6485), which projects to around $0.6380. The low from June 23 is a little below there. Yet, it would be more compelling if it had closed below the neckline, but it did not yesterday. Moreover, there was no follow-through selling and the Aussie has recovered slightly above $0.6520 today. Nevertheless, without a dramatic rally in the North American session, it will be the first weekly loss in four for the Aussie.

MXN: The US dollar remains confined to Tuesday's range against the Mexican peso. It was roughly MXN18.65 to MXN18.8850. The upper end is also the high for the month. The month's low, set July 9 near MXN18.5525, was the lowest the greenback has been since last August. The momentum indicators warn that the consolidation phase may persist a bit longer and it continues today (~MXN18.7175-MXN18.7835). Even though the attractive carry pays to be long the peso even in sideways movement, there may be some trepidation ahead of August 1 that leads to some more long liquidation. Barring a setback in the dollar today below around MXN18.6370, it will be the first back-to-back gain for the greenback since early April.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Amazon: Another Leg Higher Should Come Soon (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

You're reading my 15th article on Amazon (AMZN) here on Seeking Alpha, and while I was changing my rating from "Buy" to "Hold" and back, since early 2024, I've been constantly bullish on the stock, and it paid off big because since then AMZN has outperformed the S&P 500 index (SP500) (SPY) by ~15%.

In my previous article on AMZN, which came out in mid-May, I argued that the company's FCF dip is likely temporary, and as the balance sheet remains in solid condition, everything should be alright for Amazon, given its moat and growth opportunities across the entire ecosystem it created.

According to Seeking Alpha, Amazon reports its fiscal Q2 FY2025 by the end of this month (on July 31st, 2025, post-market). From what I see right now, it seems to me that the earnings revisions Wall Street analysts made in recent weeks are just too pessimistic, thus creating a nice low bar for beating this time. So, if it's done, the stock is very likely to keep rising higher from here, and I'd not question seemingly high valuation multiples because the stock isn't that expensive on the FWD basis, in my view.

Why Do I Think So?

As I noted in my last article, with Q1 sales of $155.67 billion (+8.62% YoY) and a 20% YoY surge in EBIT for the quarter, Amazon's non-GAAP EPS surged by 62% YoY, beating the consensus by over 16.7%, according to Seeking Alpha. All this is old news now, but it's crucial to remind ourselves about the past results at least briefly as we're coming to the Q2 print.

With NA and International revenues surging by just 8% YoY each, Amazon had yet another strong quarter in its AWS division, which showed a 17% YoY expansion, with EBIT in this segment going up to ~$11.5 billion at ~40% margin. AWS's backlog went up by 20% YoY to ~$189 billion with a weighted average contract life of ~4.1 years, and it brought in more earnings visibility to the table, in my opinion. This is an extremely profitable business, and what's also adding to the likely continuation of AMZN's consolidated margin expansion in the future is their Ads business, which is now at just 9% of the revenue mix, but it's growing fast.

Now let's move to the Q2 projections. The management guided for ~$161.5 billion in sales for the quarter (at the middle of the range), but the range appears a bit wide to me, with the higher end being at $164 billion. Given Amazon's track record of beating the past 3 years (excluding the FY2022 misses), it's actually quite interesting to see that the current consensus is focusing on the midpoint of the management's guidance, giving it just a slight premium:

We know that AMZN's Q2 guidance regarding EBIT was a bit conservative due to some one-off costs, such as ~$900 million in Project Kuiper launch costs and a seasonal step-up in SBCs, but we should also keep in mind that their operational focus on fulfillment efficiency has been paying off so far. That's what helped them beat last time, and as they keep setting new delivery speed records and expanding the same-day and next-day delivery footprint, I think we'll likely see downward-sloping cost-to-serve and thus higher margins (again).

And we also shouldn't forget that AWS's AI business is now running at a multi-billion-dollar annual run rate, which is eating away a larger share of the consolidated revenue mix, growing high double-digits on a YoY basis. AWS is becoming more and more independent with its custom-made chips, which should "reduce AI training costs and provide an alternative to Nvidia's expensive graphics processing units, or GPUs", per AWS Senior Director, Gadi Hutt's commentary.

The custom chip strategy kind of de-risks chip supply constraints and risks, which were initially expected to ease in 2H 2025, and I believe the management will address it in more detail on the upcoming Q2 earnings call.

Management highlighted ongoing chip supply constraints and power limitations affecting AWS growth rates. These are expected to ease in the second half of 2025.

Source: Seeking Alpha's Earnings Call Insights - AMZN's Q1

It's too early to tell that AWS's Trainium2 or Trainium3 will be competing with Nvidia (NVDA) or AMD (AMD) directly on the market, but I think it's definitely the right step towards AI infrastructure independence for Amazon. And it's very important to secure such independence and further get a wider moat in the market, whose size is projected in trillions over the next decade.

When it comes to the upcoming Q2, I think the bar is set too low for Amazon. First, I've already noted above that the revenue consensus is set at just a slight premium to the midpoint of the management's guidance, and given the rising share of AWS and Ads with their superior sales growth rates, the odds of beating on the top line are quite high. Second, when we turn to the EPS consensus figures, we see that over the past 3 months, the Q2 estimate was lowered by almost 7%:

As far as I know, AMZN's management team has a track record of sandbagging guidance, especially when one-off costs are in play, and the Q2 negative revision can be explained by the guided $900 million in Kuiper launch costs for the quarter. But then why do we see a few more cuts to Q3/Q4 FY2025, and even beyond that?

Also, investors should keep in mind that Amazon's Kuiper launch isn't a waste of money - it can potentially open up new revenue streams and further diversify the firm's operations. Here's how BofA analysts see this from their side [proprietary source, July 2025]:

A big opportunity, and strategic importance as well There are 2.6bn people globally who don’t have broadband internet access, and BCG sees a $40bn 2030 revenue potential for the global satellite communications service market. Based on our market assumptions, and assuming Amazon has just a 30% consumer share (Starlink already has 6mn subs), we see a $7.1bn 2032 rev. potential for Amazon. We also expect Kuiper to have synergies with Amazon’s logistic network, and the project to generate additional revenues from AWS’s enterprise and government customers. Overall, we see a compelling opportunity, and potentially strong long-term margins given high start-up costs.

Source: BofA [proprietary source, July 2025]

In addition to that, I think that Amazon's forward-buying and inventory pull-forward ahead of potential tariff hikes means that the Q2 results won't show a major margin hit from tariffs. So, it's adding to the probability of a Q2 beat to the bottom line. With more AI hardware coming online during the quarter, AWS can potentially onboard more high-margin AI workloads, so this also would mean more revenue growth at higher margins.

What caught my eye was the longer-term consensus estimates revisions, which got only words for Amazon over the past 6 months, despite the fact that a) the company showed double-beating results over the past 3 quarters in a row, and b) the AI demand isn't going anywhere (so far).

In my view, AMZN is one of the most obvious candidates for beating among mega caps during this earnings season. And while it might seem that AMZN is too expensive, I don't think so - the forward multiples show that it's likely worth buying AMZN because its high-growth business cycle has been prolonged.

I think they can keep the 30-35x earnings multiple for the next few years if the growth rates are further boosted by the new AWS's AI-related projects, Ads, and Kuiper.

Assuming a 25% EPS growth in FY2026 (vs the consensus growth rate of only 17%), and assigning 32.5x to that, I get a fair value of $252.7, which is ~13% above the current stock price. BofA analysts see it a bit differently, but they come to a similar PT:

Our $248 PO is based on our SOTP analysis that values AWS at 11x 2026 sales, 1P Retail at 1.0x, 3P Retail at 2.5x, and Advertising at 4.0x. Our 2026 sales multiples compare to 13.6x for SaaS comps, 1.3x for Retail comps, 2.6x for Marketplace comps, and 4.8x for Online Media comps. Our PO implies 3.5x blended P/Sales, a multiple at the high-end of Amazon's historical range of 1.0-3.5x, 14x '26 EBITDA, and 36x '26 EPS

Source: BofA [proprietary source, July 2025]

Where Can I Be Wrong?

We may be approaching a major turnaround in macro conditions over the next few years, with all the protectionism measures taken by different countries against each other. I can't say how badly Amazon will be impacted because of that, or how well it can cope with that. But I just look at AMZN's past success and hope that this time isn't different. If my general positive assessment of the firm's ability to overcome this kind of macro uncertainty is off, then my other assumptions will be off, too.

My valuation calculation may be too optimistic. And even with potentially optimistic inputs such as P/E above 30x or EPS growth rate well above the consensus, the stock's fair value arrives at only 13% above the current price. I mean, the upside I've calculated above may be insufficient for GARP investors. And even if AMZN beats again, there's no guarantee that the price action will be favorable after the fact.

The Bottom Line

I know it might seem illogical to buy AMZN stock on its highs and at its current valuation, but I think investors should be long-term thinkers who look beyond just 2025 and 2026. Over the next few years, I think Amazon is very likely to keep its current moat in place, and its recently launched projects should eventually be monetized, thus giving additional revenue growth acceleration. If all this comes true, then I see no reason to call AMZN an overvalued stock.

The Street seems to disagree with my bullish assessment regarding the firm's EPS expansion. In particular, the Q2 consensus figures seem to be too low, and I believe Amazon will be able to exceed them relatively easily.

That's why I keep my Buy rating in place for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: 2 Deep Value Dividend Stocks Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
Introduction

To me, it feels like I have never written more "Big Picture" articles about market-moving developments than this year. This may be partially because it's such an unusual year, as we are dealing with a wide mix of factors, including:

  • Almost daily tariff/trade war headlines.
  • The ongoing war in Ukraine, Israel, and new geopolitical risks in Asia.
  • Artificial intelligence companies continue to benefit from rapid innovation.
  • Loosening financial conditions despite no rate cuts.
  • The market's lofty valuation pressures the risk/reward.
  • An elevated term premium on U.S. government debt.
  • No clear path toward a lower government budget deficit.
  • Will Verstappen sign an F1 contract with Mercedes-AMG?

I'm obviously joking about the last bullet point.

However, my point is that I have never written more words than in 2025, as the average length of my articles is way higher than usual. Sometimes this leads to me discussing the same companies on a rather consistent basis.

That's not a bad thing, as I believe sticking to a few core stocks that are perfect for what's ahead is one of the best strategies to build wealth.

However, in addition to providing Big Picture research and actionable ideas, I'm always looking to expand my watchlist and coverage.

That's what this article is about, as I recently discovered two companies I have never discussed before.

Furthermore, I believe the vast majority of my readers will not be familiar with these companies. While these stocks tend to get regular coverage, it mainly seems to reach people who are actively searching for these companies.

Or, to put it differently, I believe they are deserving of a lot more attention.

Additionally, they both align with my outlook, meaning they benefit from favorable secular growth factors and macro tailwinds that could make them excellent investments for both growth and income.

I also have to admit that I have been really looking forward to writing this article, as the companies are quite fascinating.

So, let's get right to it!

#1: North American Construction Group (NOA) - A Fast-Growing Machinery/Energy Hybrid With Big Potential

I love Caterpillar (CAT).

It's a company I held until I was (somewhat) forced to sell it a few weeks ago due to free up cash for my Albania investment (as I discuss in this article).

However, I also like some of its biggest customers, companies that buy huge numbers of heavy-duty equipment with the goal of maximizing utilization rates through advanced rental operations.

This includes United Rentals (URI) and Toromont Industries (TIH:CA), two companies I'm bullish on that I have discussed in prior articles. These players have figured out how to squeeze the most money out of every piece of equipment, while generating tremendous shareholder value along the way.

The North American Construction Group is similar.

Headquartered in Alberta, Canada, the company has a 70-year history of delivering services to the mining, resource, and industrial construction sectors, with a market cap of just $500 million.

As we can see below, the company specializes in providing heavy equipment services, mine management, and civil construction for large-scale projects, especially in the oil sands, critical minerals, and infrastructure markets.

Moreover, the company, which is often abbreviated as NACG, operates through three main segments: Heavy Equipment - Canada, Heavy Equipment - Australia, and Other. The "other" part includes mine management contracts in the U.S. and external maintenance programs.

While this is a competitive industry, the company has five benefits that create higher barriers for new entrants:

  • Building a fleet of high-quality equipment is very expensive. While capital isn't a huge barrier for many, ordering equipment can take a long time, especially in times of elevated demand. Having a good relationship with a producer like Caterpillar is key.
  • Without a strong maintenance network, competing is impossible.
  • Operating in areas like Canada and Australia requires strong connections with indigenous partners and governments.
  • Having a strong safety track record can help onboard new business.
  • Operating in harsh conditions is different from providing equipment for random construction projects.

With that said, as most of my readers may know because I frequently cover Canadian oil and gas companies, Canada has some of the world's biggest fossil fuel reserves in the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin. It's home to billions of barrels of untapped reserves, providing oil companies with decades of high-quality inventory, excluding any new discoveries.

That's also why I expect Canada to boost its production for many years to come, assuming it gets new pipeline projects soon, as the Trans Mountain Pipeline alone cannot deal with the rising supply. Especially in light of the end of the American shale revolution, Canada is a prime spot for higher output.

I'm bringing this up because Canada's oil sands are unusual, as oil is mined using trucks instead of the onshore rigs used in areas like Texas. According to the Government of Alberta, trucks and excavators transport oil sands to facilities where these sands are mixed with hot water and diluents to separate the bitumen from the sand. According to the company, in order to produce 110 thousand barrels of oil per day, one needs:

  • 100x 400-ton trucks.
  • 9x 800-ton loaders.
  • 18x 600-HP dozers.
  • 8x 500-HP graders.

While these operations come with elevated upfront costs, they tend to provide producers with ultra-low breakeven costs.

In these oil sands, the company has more than 200 large-capacity assets, including the equivalent of 80 400T-sized trucks that move roughly 120 million tons per year, with a utilization rate expected to return to 75% by year-end.

It also helps that the company has some of the best partners one can wish for, including Suncor Energy, Syncrude, Imperial Oil, Canadian Natural, IAMGOLD, De Beers Group, BHP, Pilbara Minerals, Anglo American, and Fortescue.

It also bought the MacKellar Group in 2023, an Australian leasing giant with a market share of less than 10% in a market that is 10x the size of Canada, meaning there are tremendous growth opportunities, both organic and M&A.

Australia, which is now its biggest market and most favorable growth market, not only provides tremendous opportunities because of its fractionated market, but also because Australia's metals benefit from secular growth, including electrification, and a booming gold and silver industry.

Even better, secular growth in infrastructure from the energy transition and resource independence trends (fueled by the trade war) has triggered NACG to have the goal to grow infrastructure revenues to 25% of its operations within three years.

Excluding M&A, the company aims for annual growth of 5% to 10%, fueled by the displacement of higher-cost contractors in Australia, infrastructure projects, and growth in various areas in Canada.

Moreover, because of aggressive investments in the past, the company now expects to accelerate its EPS through incremental project wins, which do not require a substantial boost in capital investments, somewhat that benefit earnings, margins, and free cash flow, among other financial metrics.

Since 2018, the company has grown its EBITDA by 20% per year and boosted its EPS by more than 4.0x, with support from both strong post-pandemic demand and M&A.

Additionally, despite aggressive M&A, the company aims for a net leverage ratio of 1.7x at the end of this year, a very healthy number providing investors and the company with stability in what has become an uncertain funding environment.

Furthermore, although the company has experienced more pressure on its margins than two of its competitors (note that United Rentals is not focused on mining), post-M&A synergies and additional project wins should significantly boost margins.

With regard to its dividend, North American Construction yields 2.2%. Its dividend is paid in Canadian dollars, which means non-Canadian investors are prone to currency risks.

Moreover, the dividend is much more attractive than its 2.2% yield suggests, as it has a payout ratio of just 13% and a five-year CAGR of 24%, meaning it has the dividend growth rate of an ultra-low-yielding company and a history of allowing investors to benefit from rapid growth in the past.

Valuation-wise, the company trades at a mere 8.6x earnings, a mile below its long-term average of 15.1x, as the market hasn't been too keen to bet on cyclical value stocks. However, as I have written in prior articles, including this one, I believe it's a great risk/reward place to be.

Even based on current EPS expectations (FactSet) and an 11.5x multiple, there's room for Toronto-based shares to rise to C$35, roughly 60% above the current price. This also goes for New York-listed shares.

If I did not already have substantial energy exposure, I would be a buyer of NOA stock, as I think it's a terrific value play.

However, it's volatile and very cyclical. As bullish as I am, please be aware of these risks when doing your own due diligence.

The next pick is also a company I have never discussed.

#2: First Industrial Realty Trust (FR) - A Fast-Growing Industrial REIT I Wish I Had Found Sooner

Economic re-shoring is one of my favorite Big Picture trends. What started during President Trump's first term has become a "project" of common interest in the nation's capital, as both the Biden Administration and Trump's two administrations have been working on ways to bring back value-adding production, including manufacturing, high-tech chip manufacturing, healthcare, and so much more.

This is one of the reasons why I like industrial real estate. Without sufficient infrastructure, economic re-shoring isn't happening.

Personally, I own Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), an industrial REIT with 100% exposure to Southern California's infill market, the most favorable market in the nation when it comes to addressing America's biggest consumer and industrial bases, and because it has the lowest supply risks.

Despite the political risks of SoCal, the fact that I was so satisfied with REXR (I still am) caused me to ignore opportunities in other areas, which is why I am including First Industrial Realty Trust in this article.

First Industrial has a $7 billion market cap, roughly $2 billion less than Rexford, with an emphasis on 15 key logistics markets with "coastal orientation," which means high demand in favorable corridors. As we can see below, this includes Southern California, where roughly 26% of its rental revenue is generated, making it the company's largest market.

On top of that, the company, which has 874 total tenants and a large focus on infill assets, has high exposure to the Northeast, South Florida, and Sunbelt Markets like Dallas-Fort-Worth and Houston. Its total occupancy rate is 95.3%, which is roughly in line with some of its bigger peers.

Moreover, with regard to tariff risks, the company used its latest earnings call to note that it has minimal exposure to Chinese third-party logistics ("3PL") tenants, with only 450,000 square feet leased to these companies.

It also has no significant concerns from auto tenants, who are mainly involved in design and assembly instead of heavy manufacturing. And even if they were, I am quite bullish on automotive production in the U.S., although it's a slow and gradual trend due to the capital-intensive nature of the automotive industry.

In general, the good news is that CoStar data shows stability in the U.S. industrial market, with recovering occupancy rates and favorable new construction. Thanks to higher rates, construction has been under pressure since 2022, potentially paving the way for higher rent growth going forward.

This also bodes well for First Industrial. Although its occupancy rate has declined since 2022 due to unfavorable industry fundamentals, the occupancy rate has been stable since 2023.

Moreover, mark-to-market rent adjustments provide elevated growth.

In 1Q25, for example, cash rental rate growth was strong, with a 30% increase for new and renewal leases (36% excluding a Central PA fixed-rate renewal). For the full year, First Industrial expects cash rental rate growth of 30% to 40% (35% to 45% excluding the fixed-rate renewal), which obviously reflects strong pricing power due to elevated market rent growth in the past.

In order to grow, the company mainly engages in large projects in land-constrained areas with the ability to build-to-suit and to find the right assets that work for single and multi-tenant partners. Since 2016, the company's developments have created $1.3 billion in value with a cash yield of 7.0%.

Currently, the company has seven projects with expected completions between 2Q25 and 1Q26. These are 43% leased as of March 31 with an estimated cash yield of 7.1% and an estimated potential development margin of up to 44%.

All things considered, the company looks for opportunities in other areas:

  • E-commerce and logistics demand: Continued growth in e-commerce and nearshoring trends drives demand for warehouses in great locations, especially in markets like the Inland Empire, where large leases are active.
  • Supply constraints: A 75% decline in new construction starts since 3Q22 limits competitive supply, supporting rental rate growth and occupancy stability.
  • Strategic developments: Projects in high-demand submarkets with 8% yields offer attractive risk-adjusted returns.
  • Market Expansion: Ongoing investments in high-growth regions like Texas, Florida, and Pennsylvania align with long-term industrial demand trends.

These developments are supported by a very healthy balance sheet, with a net debt ratio of 5.2x, which includes preferred equity. It has close to zero in secured debt and no maturities in 2025 and 2026. Earlier this year, it placed $450 million in Senior Notes due 2031 for 5.25%, which is a good deal, given the yields on non-risk government debt.

It has an investment-grade credit rating of BBB.

Regarding its dividend, the company pays $1.78 annually, resulting in a yield of 3.6%. This dividend has a five-year CAGR of 11.2%, one of the highest in the REIT sector, which has a median of just 1.2%.

Note that the company cut its dividend during the Great Financial Crisis. It resumed in 2012. Since then, it has massively improved its business, allowing the company to even hike its dividend during the pandemic.

In fact, since reinstating the dividend in 2012, it has been hiked every year, protected by a healthy balance sheet and a 76% dividend payout ratio.

Valuation-wise, the company trades at 21.5x adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO"). This year, per-share AFFO growth is expected to be 7%, potentially followed by 5% and 12% growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively.

Although it will likely require lower interest rates and a confirmation of an industrial rebound to push the valuation back to 25.4x, the company has a clear path to a $70 stock price target, 40% above the current price.

Takeaway

This year has been packed with big-picture market themes, including trade wars, geopolitical risks, AI breakthroughs, and the ongoing deficit issue, which is why my articles have been longer than usual.

However, as I explained, while deep dives into core holdings are valuable, I'm always hunting for fresh opportunities.

That's what this article was all about, as I presented North American Construction Group and First Industrial Realty Trust, two under-the-radar names with serious potential.

  • NOA is what I like to call a machinery/energy hybrid with a dominant position in Canadian oil sands and Australian mining. It's cheap at 8.6x earnings, pays a growing 2.2% yield, and has a clear path to 60% upside as macro tailwinds kick in and the company should benefit from better access to highly fragmented markets.
  • FR is an industrial REIT that benefits from the re-shoring wave, with prime coastal logistics assets and elevated rent growth on new leases. Trading at a discount to peers, I believe it could surge 40% as development yields and occupancy stabilize.

I don't own any of them yet, but I put both on my watchlist.

Risks To My Thesis

NOA is a very volatile and cyclical small-cap that needs to be handled with care. While I am super bullish on it, it is the wrong company for low-volatility investors.

First Industrial is much safer, with the biggest risks being prolonged industrial real estate weakness and the inability to find tenants for new developments.

data.title: Fixed Income Investment Outlook Q3 2025
data.description:
Riding the Rates Wave

We believe the Federal Reserve is likely to soon join other central banks in further cutting interest rates, likely providing a tailwind for shorter-duration U.S. bonds.

The last few months have been eventful, to say the least, from tariffs to Middle East conflict to the passage of U.S. budget legislation, but ultimately impacts on fixed income have been fairly muted.

The Trump administration’s announcement of the tariff “Liberation Day” on April 2 inaugurated a period of extreme volatility in financial markets, trying to assess the potential levels and impacts of proposed U.S. levies on imports. Postponement until July (and then August) of more severe tariffs helped risk assets reverse course, although the dollar continued its sharp decline amid foreign skepticism over U.S. deficit spending. In geopolitics, the sharp escalation—and swift apparent conclusion—of the Iranian conflict created a temporary spike in oil prices but otherwise had little effect on global markets. Meanwhile, the July 4th enactment of the U.S. President’s “Big, Beautiful” budget law was greeted with a mixture of relief and worry over fiscal strains, only to be overshadowed within days by new tariff threats, perceived by some as a negotiation tactic.

As is often the case, bond investors had their eye less on the commotion and more on the ultimate drivers of returns — growth and inflation. There, the overall backdrop was relatively benign, with “hard” U.S. growth data coming in better than “soft” sentiment-driven data and inflation continuing to recede even amid worries about the impact of tariffs on prices. In Europe, inflation is also fading, but growth weakness is being tempered by new spending, particularly in Germany given its new commitment to infrastructure and defense. Japan is in solid condition, and China may be on the mend, aided by policy initiatives.

Looking ahead, we see broad potential for rate cuts, particularly in the U.S., where softer near-term growth, encouraging disinflation and more labor market ambiguity could curtail the central bank’s wait-and-see stance on tariff impacts. (Current pressure on Fed leadership could conceivably prove a risk, which we are watching closely.) Easing could prove beneficial to short- to intermediate-term bonds, although longer-dated U.S. issues still face questions over deficit spending. While tariff noise may continue, the world appears likely to avoid recession, reinforcing the justification for tight corporate credit spreads. Among sectors, we think high yield bonds offer relative advantage, while emerging markets debt issued in local currencies could prove a bright spot given dollar weakness, moderate inflation and resilient growth.

We provide our key investment themes for the quarter on the following pages.

1 - U.S. Growth and Inflation are Easing, but Questions Remain

Tariffs have been a key stumbling block for corporate managements and investors this year, given the relatively extreme “opening bids” by the Trump administration and the wide range of potential outcomes for tariff levels and impacts, depending on results from negotiations across much of the globe.
